title: It Came Upon A Midnight Clear
abc_source:
X: 60
T: It Came Upon A Midnight Clear
%%combinevoices 1
C: Words: Edmund H. Sears, 1849.  Music: 'Carol' Richard S. Willis, 1861.  
C: Setting: "Order of worship for the Reformed Church in the United States", 1866.
C: copyright: public domain.  This score is a part of the Open Hymnal Project, 2005 Revision.
S: Music source: "Order of worship for the Reformed Church in the United States", 1866 Hymn 63.
S: supposed to be found in "Church Corals and Choir Studies" 1850 by Willis, but I don't see it in that book. 
S: some sources say 1850, some 1859, some 1861.  Willis was editor for "The Musical World" magazine.  Probably first appeared there.

metadata.composer: 'Carol' Richard S. Willis, 1861 "Order of worship for the Reformed Church in the United States", 1866
metadata.copyright: public domain.  This score is a part of the Open Hymnal Project, 2005 Revision.
c_lines: Words: Edmund H. Sears, 1849.  Music: 'Carol' Richard S. Willis, 1861.
Setting: "Order of worship for the Reformed Church in the United States", 1866.
copyright: public domain.  This score is a part of the Open Hymnal Project, 2005 Revision.

1.raw_text: It came upon the midnight clear, That glorious song of old, From angels bending near the earth, To touch their harps of gold; "Peace on the earth, good will to men, From Heaven's all gracious King." The world in solemn stillness lay, To hear the angels sing.

2.raw_text: Still through the cloven skies they come With peaceful wings unfurled, And still their heavenly music floats O'er all the weary world; Above its sad and lowly plains, They bend on hov'ring wing, And ever over its Babel sounds The blessèd angels sing.

3.raw_text: Yet with the woes of sin and strife The world has suffered long; Beneath the angel strain have rolled Two thousand years of wrong; And man, at war with man, hears not The love\- song which they bring; O hush the noise, ye men of strife And hear the angels sing.

4.raw_text: And ye, beneath life's crushing load, Whose forms are bending low, Who toil along the climbing way With painful steps and slow, Look now! for glad and golden hours Come swiftly on the wing. O rest beside the weary road, And hear the angels sing!

5.raw_text: For lo! the days are hast'ning on, By prophet\- bards foretold, When with the ever circling years Comes round the age of gold; When peace shall over all the earth Its ancient splendors fling, And the whole world send back the song Which now the angels sing.
